full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I vividly remember in the first parish where I served as rector, we had a doctor, Dr. Bill.<|6.70|> <|7.42|> This was at St. John's Episcopal Church in Decatur, Alabama, and Bill served one of the local hospitals, and he often covered the psych ward.<|17.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I vividly remember him telling me, after this very gospel reading was read in church, telling me at the door, he said, I can tell you stories about people who've taken this gospel too literally.<|15.04|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.18|> Jesus here is, as he often does, is being hyperbolic.<|6.42|> <|6.56|> He's making a very dramatic point.<|9.24|> <|10.64|> And as you've heard Broderick, Katie, and myself say, as many Episcopal priests and Christians<|17.12|> <|17.12|> and preachers of many wonderfully thoughtful denominations say, we take the Bible too seriously<|22.72|> <|22.72|> to take it literally sometimes.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is a case in point.<|3.50|> <|3.50|> Jesus is certainly getting our attention, but here he's not being literal.<|12.72|> <|12.72|> The word for hell is really interesting.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.00|> Some scholars, well, it comes from a place and not a metaphysical place.<|7.40|> <|7.50|> It comes from the Valley of Gehenna.<|9.38|> <|10.02|> And some scholars believe that was the place outside the gates of Jerusalem,<|14.48|> <|14.66|> outside the city proper, where trash was burned, other things were burned.<|19.18|> <|19.88|> It was a place, therefore, that had a range of incredible psychological associations,<|24.74|> <|24.74|> as you might understand.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> imagine. And Jesus therefore uses it here as an apt metaphor for what within<|6.60|> <|6.60|> ourselves needs to be let go of, what needs to be burned and turned away from.<|13.88|> <|13.88|> Last week we had in the Dean's Forum Dr. Adam Gregerman, a Jewish scholar from St.<|19.44|> <|19.44|> Joseph's in Philadelphia, and he and I for some reason started talking about this very word<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And he pointed out that the Valley of Gehenna,<|2.44|> <|2.44|> and I didn't know this today,<|3.54|> <|3.54|> is one of the most beautiful parts in all of Israel.<|6.88|> <|6.88|> A valley with grass and the place where people go<|9.28|> <|9.28|> to eat and to reflect, a place of beauty in other words.<|14.28|> <|14.28|> And I think that helps complete the circle,<|17.38|> <|17.38|> tie the loop together about what's going on here.<|21.02|> <|21.02|> We need to get rid of our sins, our mistakes,<|24.52|> <|24.52|> our unhealthy patterns.<|26.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> so that, with the grace of God, we become a more authentic and beautiful and true version of ourselves.<|7.90|> <|9.04|> Katie Pearson, Ken and Katie Pearson, loves the word, and will occasionally use the word, truer.<|15.06|> <|16.06|> And I think what this metaphor is about is always about becoming a truer version of mind.<|9.56|> <|9.56|> A fanatical mind can't receive any input beyond itself.<|15.40|> <|15.40|> And I want to say one more thing that's urgent.<|18.70|> <|18.70|> We often think of religious of his argument, that Christianity should always do the hard and laborious work of connecting<|18.18|> <|18.18|> with other fields of knowledge, of checking in with worlds beyond itself.<|24.08|>
